nativeRange.habitat: Outer reef slopes; forms dense aggregations on steep reef walls at depths typically from 1 to 55 m, most commonly encountered between 1 and 35 m

diet.notes: Feeds on zooplankton in the wild. In captivity, generally does best on enriched frozen mysis shrimp and enriched frozen brine shrimp offered multiple times per day in small quantities; has been reported to eventually accept high-quality flake foods. An attached refugium cultivating copepods and amphipods is recommended to supplement feeding and aid acclimation.

summary: A small, peaceful Indo-Pacific anthias generally kept in groups of five or more on reef slopes. Known for protogynous hermaphroditism and relatively demanding acclimation needs; generally recommended for experienced reef keepers.
careNotes: Waitei Anthias tend to be shy, especially when first introduced, and benefit from a well-established aquarium with plenty of live rock providing caves and hiding places alongside open mid-water swimming areas. Groups of five or more individuals are generally advised, as solitary specimens often fare poorly. Like other anthias, members are protogynous hermaphrodites — if the dominant male is lost, the largest female in the group has been known to transition into a functioning male. Feeding multiple small meals throughout the day is recommended due to their limited stomach capacity; a refugium producing copepods and amphipods can significantly ease acclimation. LiveAquaria rates care as 'Difficult', consistent with advanced-level placement here. Tank size recommendation of 500 L is based on the group-of-five minimum and the species' active mid-water schooling behavior and need for open swimming space; a 55-gallon minimum cited by LiveAquaria is considered a bare minimum for a single specimen and insufficient for a proper harem group. Cross-source temperature disagreement noted: LiveAquaria gives 22–26°C, FishBase 22–28°C, and TankFacts 24–27°C; averaged range of 23–27°C used.
breedingNotes: No captive breeding information is available in the literature surveyed. As protogynous hermaphrodites, males develop from the largest female in a group when the dominant male is absent. Purchasing a group and allowing natural sex-change to occur is the recommended approach for hobbyists interested in breeding.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (7.0 cm TL), temperature range (22–28°C), native distribution (Indo-Pacific from Seychelles to Loyalty Islands and Japan), family (Anthiadidae), and habitat (reef-associated, dense zooplankton-feeding aggregations). FishBase max temperature of 28°C is higher than LiveAquaria's 78°F (25.6°C); averaged with TankFacts to produce 27°C max.

additional.name: TankFacts
additional.notes: Third cross-check source; provided temperature range 76–80°F (24.4–26.7°C), pH 7.8–8.4, salinity 1.023–1.026, and adult size up to 7 cm. Minor disagreement on pH minimum (7.8 vs LiveAquaria 8.1); averaged to 8.0. Temperature minimum of 76°F (24.4°C) is higher than LiveAquaria/FishBase; three-source average used.

fish.breedingNotes: No documented captive breeding; protogynous hermaphrodites that naturally sex-change within established groups when the dominant male is lost.
